cwe: CWE-338 - Use of Cryptographically Weak Pseudo-Random Number Generator (PRNG)
CWE-330 - Use of Insufficiently Random Values

threat.title: Predictable Token Generation via Weak Random Number Generators
threat.description: When applications use weak pseudo-random number generators (PRNGs) like Math.random(), java.util.Random, or random.randint() for security-critical operations such as session tokens, password reset tokens, or API keys, attackers can predict future values and forge authentication tokens. This allows complete account takeover without knowing user credentials.

attack.scenario:
1. Attacker creates multiple user accounts and requests password reset tokens
2. Analyzes the generated tokens to identify weak randomness patterns (e.g., Math.random() seeded with timestamp)
3. Reverse-engineers the PRNG state using known token values and timing information
4. Predicts future token values for target victim account
5. Initiates password reset for victim account and intercepts/predicts the reset token
6. Uses predicted token to reset victim password and gain full account access
prerequisites: Application uses weak PRNG (Math.random(), java.util.Random, random.randint())
Tokens used for authentication or password reset are generated with weak randomness
Attacker can observe multiple generated tokens to identify patterns
No additional entropy sources or token binding mechanisms in place

cve_references.description: Apache Struts 2 used weak random number generation for CSRF tokens, allowing attackers to predict token values
Apache Tomcat used predictable session IDs due to weak randomness in Windows environments
Realtek SDK used weak random number generation for WPS PIN, allowing brute force attacks

real_world_breaches.impact: Millions of SSH keys, SSL certificates, and cryptographic keys generated with predictable randomness over 2 years
real_world_breaches.root_cause: OpenSSL PRNG seeded with only process ID, reducing entropy from 2^128 to ~32,000 possible keys

real_world_breaches.impact: Multiple PHP applications vulnerable to session prediction due to mt_rand() used for session IDs
real_world_breaches.root_cause: mt_rand() is a Mersenne Twister PRNG not suitable for cryptographic purposes, predictable after observing outputs

bug_bounty_reports.summary: Password reset token prediction via Math.random() allowing account takeover on Fortune 500 e-commerce platform

security_research.title: Attacking PRNGs: Breaking Session IDs and Password Reset Tokens
security_research.key_finding: Predictable randomness in session tokens is one of the most exploited vulnerabilities, with attackers needing only 10-20 observations to predict future values

security_research.title: NIST SP 800-90A: Recommendation for Random Number Generation Using Deterministic Random Bit Generators
security_research.key_finding: Cryptographically secure PRNGs must be used for all security-sensitive operations; standard library PRNGs are unsuitable

mitigations.title: Use crypto.randomBytes() for Secure Token Generation in Node.js
mitigations.description: Replace Math.random() with Node.js crypto.randomBytes() which provides cryptographically secure random values from the operating system's CSPRNG (/dev/urandom on Unix, CryptGenRandom on Windows).
iso27001_controls: A.10.1.1 - Policy on the use of cryptographic controls
nist_csf: PR.DS-5 - Protections against data leaks are implemented
PR.AC-1 - Identities and credentials are managed
validation_steps: Replace all Math.random() calls used for security purposes with crypto.randomBytes()
Ensure minimum 128 bits (16 bytes) of entropy for session tokens
Use 256 bits (32 bytes) for password reset tokens and API keys
Store hashed tokens in database, never plaintext
Implement token expiration and single-use mechanisms
Test with statistical randomness tests (NIST test suite)

mitigations.title: Use SecureRandom for Token Generation in Java/Spring Boot
mitigations.description: Replace java.util.Random with java.security.SecureRandom which provides cryptographically strong random values. Use SecureRandom for all security-sensitive operations including session IDs, CSRF tokens, and password resets.
iso27001_controls: A.10.1.1 - Policy on the use of cryptographic controls
nist_csf: PR.AC-1 - Identities and credentials are managed
validation_steps: Replace all java.util.Random with SecureRandom for security operations
Use SecureRandom.getInstanceStrong() or NativePRNGNonBlocking algorithm
Generate minimum 128 bits (16 bytes) for session tokens
Use 256 bits (32 bytes) for password reset tokens
Store SHA-256 hashed tokens in database
Implement token expiration and single-use logic
Run NIST statistical test suite for randomness validation

mitigations.title: Use secrets Module or os.urandom() in Python
mitigations.description: Replace random.randint() and random.random() with the secrets module (Python 3.6+) or os.urandom() which provide cryptographically secure random values from /dev/urandom on Unix systems.

code_example.code:
import secrets
import hashlib
import os
from datetime import datetime, timedelta
from flask import Flask, request, jsonify

app = Flask(__name__)

# VULNERABLE CODE - DO NOT USE
def generate_weak_token():
    import random
    return str(random.randint(100000, 999999))  # Predictable!

# SECURE: Generate session token
def generate_session_token():
    """Generate 256-bit cryptographically secure session token."""
    return secrets.token_urlsafe(32)  # 32 bytes = 256 bits

# SECURE: Generate password reset token
def generate_password_reset_token():
    """Generate secure password reset token with hash."""
    token = secrets.token_urlsafe(32)
    token_hash = hashlib.sha256(token.encode()).hexdigest()
    expires_at = datetime.utcnow() + timedelta(hours=1)
    
    return {
        'token': token,
        'token_hash': token_hash,
        'expires_at': expires_at
    }

# SECURE: Generate API key with prefix
def generate_api_key():
    """Generate API key with service prefix."""
    random_part = secrets.token_urlsafe(24)
    return f"sk_{random_part}"

# SECURE: Generate numeric OTP (one-time password)
def generate_otp(length=6):
    """Generate cryptographically secure numeric OTP."""
    # Use secrets.randbelow for uniform distribution
    return ''.join(str(secrets.randbelow(10)) for _ in range(length))

# SECURE: Generate hex token
def generate_hex_token(num_bytes=32):
    """Generate hex-encoded token."""
    return secrets.token_hex(num_bytes)

# Alternative: Using os.urandom directly
def generate_token_urandom():
    """Generate token using os.urandom (lower-level approach)."""
    import base64
    random_bytes = os.urandom(32)
    return base64.urlsafe_b64encode(random_bytes).decode('utf-8').rstrip('=')

# Example: Password reset endpoint
@app.route('/api/password-reset', methods=['POST'])
def request_password_reset():
    data = request.get_json()
    email = data.get('email')
    
    # Find user (pseudo-code)
    user = db.users.find_one({'email': email})
    
    if not user:
        # Prevent user enumeration - return generic message
        return jsonify({'message': 'If account exists, reset email sent'}), 200
    
    # Generate secure token
    reset_data = generate_password_reset_token()
    
    # Store hashed token in database (NEVER store plaintext)
    db.password_resets.insert_one({
        'user_id': user['_id'],
        'token_hash': reset_data['token_hash'],
        'expires_at': reset_data['expires_at'],
        'used': False,
        'created_at': datetime.utcnow()
    })
    
    # Send email with token (use HTTPS link)
    send_email(
        to=email,
        subject='Password Reset Request',
        body=f"Reset link: https://example.com/reset-password?token={reset_data['token']}"
    )
    
    return jsonify({'message': 'If account exists, reset email sent'}), 200

# Verify password reset token
@app.route('/api/password-reset/verify', methods=['POST'])
def verify_password_reset():
    data = request.get_json()
    token = data.get('token')
    new_password = data.get('new_password')
    
    # Hash provided token
    token_hash = hashlib.sha256(token.encode()).hexdigest()
    
    # Find valid reset request
    reset_request = db.password_resets.find_one({
        'token_hash': token_hash,
        'used': False,
        'expires_at': {'$gt': datetime.utcnow()}
    })
    
    if not reset_request:
        return jsonify({'error': 'Invalid or expired token'}), 400
    
    # Mark token as used (single-use only)
    db.password_resets.update_one(
        {'_id': reset_request['_id']},
        {'$set': {'used': True}}
    );
    
    # Update password with bcrypt/argon2
    from werkzeug.security import generate_password_hash
    password_hash = generate_password_hash(new_password, method='pbkdf2:sha256', salt_length=16)
    
    db.users.update_one(
        {'_id': reset_request['user_id']},
        {'$set': {'password_hash': password_hash}}
    )
    
    return jsonify({'message': 'Password reset successful'}), 200

# Django example
from django.http import JsonResponse
from django.views.decorators.csrf import csrf_exempt
import json

@csrf_exempt
def django_password_reset(request):
    if request.method == 'POST':
        data = json.loads(request.body)
        email = data.get('email')
        
        # Generate secure token using secrets module
        reset_token = generate_password_reset_token()
        
        # Store in Django ORM
        from myapp.models import PasswordReset
        PasswordReset.objects.create(
            email=email,
            token_hash=reset_token['token_hash'],
            expires_at=reset_token['expires_at']
        )
        
        return JsonResponse({'message': 'Reset email sent'})

if __name__ == '__main__':
    # Test token generation
    print("Session Token:", generate_session_token())
    print("API Key:", generate_api_key())
    print("OTP:", generate_otp())
    print("Hex Token:", generate_hex_token())

iso27001_controls: A.10.1.1 - Policy on the use of cryptographic controls
nist_csf: PR.AC-1 - Identities and credentials are managed
validation_steps: Replace all random.randint(), random.random(), random.choice() with secrets module
Use secrets.token_urlsafe() for URL-safe tokens
Use secrets.token_hex() for hex-encoded tokens
Use secrets.token_bytes() for raw bytes
Generate minimum 128 bits (16 bytes) for session tokens
Use 256 bits (32 bytes) for password reset tokens and API keys
Store SHA-256 hashed tokens in database
Test with NIST statistical randomness test suite

indicators: Multiple password reset requests from same IP in short time window
Password reset token successfully used shortly after generation (potential prediction)
Session tokens with observable patterns or sequential values
High number of failed token validation attempts followed by successful access
API keys with predictable prefixes and short random portions
Multiple account takeovers without credential theft indicators

log_queries.query:
// Detect rapid password reset token usage (potential prediction)
CustomLogs
| where EventType == "PasswordResetTokenGenerated" or EventType == "PasswordResetTokenUsed"
| summarize 
    TokenGenerated = countif(EventType == "PasswordResetTokenGenerated"),
    TokenUsed = countif(EventType == "PasswordResetTokenUsed"),
    TimeDiff = max(TimeGenerated) - min(TimeGenerated)
    by UserId, bin(TimeGenerated, 5m)
| where TokenUsed > 0 and TimeDiff < 2m // Token used within 2 minutes
| extend Severity = "High"
| project TimeGenerated, UserId, TokenGenerated, TokenUsed, TimeDiff, Severity

// Detect multiple password reset attempts from same IP
event.category: "authentication" AND event.action: "password_reset_requested"
| stats count by source.ip, user.email
| where count > 5

index=app_logs sourcetype=auth_events
(event_type="password_reset_token_generated" OR event_type="password_reset_token_used")
| stats 
    count(case(event_type="password_reset_token_generated",1)) as generated,
    count(case(event_type="password_reset_token_used",1)) as used,
    range(_time) as time_range
    by user_id
| where used > 0 AND time_range < 120
| addinfo severity="high"
| table _time, user_id, generated, used, time_range, severity

// Detect token prediction attack pattern
_sourceCategory=authentication 
("password_reset_token_generated" OR "password_reset_token_used")
| parse regex "(?<event_type>password_reset_token_(generated|used)).*user_id=(?<user_id>\\w+).*token_hash=(?<token_hash>\\w+)"
| timeslice 5m
| count by _timeslice, user_id, event_type
| transpose row _timeslice column event_type as *
| where password_reset_token_used > 0
| where password_reset_token_generated > 3

tools.name: ENT (NIST Statistical Test Suite)
tools.usage: ent generated_tokens.bin to test for entropy, chi-square, and correlation

tools.name: Dieharder
tools.usage: dieharder -a -f token_samples.bin to detect weak PRNG patterns

tools.name: PRNG Cracker
tools.usage: Tools like untwister for Mersenne Twister state recovery from outputs
